1: wĕʾēlleh haggôyim ʾašer hinnîaḥ YHWH lĕnassôt bām ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — 'these are the nations YHWH left to test Israel.' The verb hinnîaḥ (hiph of nûaḥ, to leave, allow to remain) signals intentional preservation — these nations are not accidental leftovers but deliberately maintained instruments of divine testing and discipline.
2: raq lĕmaʿan daʿat dōrôt bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl lĕlammĕdām milḥāmāh — 'only so that generations of Israel might know war and be taught it.' Two purposes: covenant testing (v.4) and military education for generations with no Exodus memory. The pedagogical framing acknowledges divine sovereignty over historical hardship — no Israelite suffering is unintentional in this period.
3: The enemy roster: the five Philistine lords (sĕrānê pĕlištîm), Canaanites, Sidonians, and Hivites. Sārēn is the specific title of the Philistine pentapolis rulers — likely an Indo-European loanword reflecting the Philistines' Aegean origins. The geographic range from the Philistine coast to the Lebanon mountains maps the entire unconquered perimeter surrounding Israel's highland core.
4: wayhî lĕnassôt bām ʾet-yiśrāʾēl lādaʿat hăyišmĕʿû ʾet-miṣwôt YHWH — 'to test Israel, to find out whether they would obey the commandments.' The infinitive lādaʿat (to know, to find out) marks experiential verification — the test produces knowledge that theory cannot. The object of the test: whether šāmaʿ (obey, hear-and-heed) would operate under the specific pressure of living among unconquered peoples.
5: ûbĕnê yiśrāʾēl yāšĕbû bĕqereb hakkĕnaʿănî — 'the people of Israel lived in the midst of the Canaanites.' The preposition bĕqereb (in the midst, within) signals the conquest's reversal: instead of Canaanites being expelled from Israel's midst, Israel inhabits the Canaanite world. The seven-nation list catalogs the full breadth of the cultural-religious environment Israel chose to inhabit.
6: wayyiqqĕḥû ʾet-bĕnôtêhem lāhem lĕnāšîm... wayyaʿabdû ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem — 'they took their daughters... gave their daughters... and served their gods.' The intermarriage-idolatry chain is the exact sequence Deuteronomy 7:3-4 warned against: 'do not intermarry... for they will turn your sons away to serve other gods.' The verb chain (took, gave, served) follows Deuteronomy's predicted causality exactly.
7: wayyiškĕḥû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem — 'they forgot the LORD their God.' The verb šākaḥ (forget) in Deuteronomy describes active neglect, not innocent forgetting: Deut 8:11-19 identifies forgetting YHWH as the covenant danger of prosperity; Deut 8:19 makes it the condition for perishing. Hāʾăšērôt are the sacred wooden poles associated with Asherah, Canaanite consort of El — the female divine pillar planted in the ground as a worship object.
8: wayyiḥar-ʾap YHWH bĕyiśrāʾēl wayyimkĕrēm bĕyad kûšan-rišʿātayim — 'YHWH sold them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim.' The verb mākar (sell) is stark: Israel becomes chattel, transferred in a transaction. The king's name kûšan rišʿātayim ('Cushan of double wickedness') is almost certainly a Hebrew satirical gloss on his actual name — OT authors regularly distort enemies' names. Rišʿātayim (dual of rišʿāh) = doubly wicked, making the title a theological verdict on the oppressor.
9: wayyizʿăqû bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH wayyāqem YHWH môšîaʿ lĕbĕnê-yiśrāʾēl — 'the Israelites cried out to YHWH, and YHWH raised up a deliverer.' The verb zāʿaq (cry out, cry for help) is the vocabulary of oppressed people in extremity (Exod 3:7; Ps 34:17). The covenantal hinge: cry → raised deliverer. Môšîaʿ (participle of yāšaʿ, one who saves) is the Othniel narrative's central term and the paradigm that every subsequent judge repeats with variations.
10: wattĕhî ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH wayyišpōṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl wayyēṣēʾ lammilḥāmāh — 'the Spirit of YHWH came upon him, he judged Israel, and he went out to war.' The rûaḥ YHWH (Spirit/wind of YHWH) is the empowering presence that equips the judge. The Spirit precedes and motivates the action; the sequence — Spirit comes → judgment → warfare — establishes the theological pattern for all subsequent judge narratives. The judges' effectiveness is divine endowment, not personal charisma.
11: wayyišqōṭ hāʾāreṣ ʾarbaʿîm šānāh wayyāmot ʿotnîʾēl ben-qĕnaz — 'the land had rest forty years; then Othniel died.' Šāqaṭ (have rest, be quiet) is the Judges peace-formula after each deliverer's work. The forty-year period is the formulaic generation. Othniel's narrative is the most schematic — no personal flaws, no complications — establishing the ideal-type against which all subsequent judges are implicitly measured.
12: wayyōsipû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl laʿăśôt hāraʿ bĕʿênê YHWH — 'they again did evil.' Yāsap (again, additionally) signals iteration: the cycle restarts. wayyĕḥazzēq YHWH ʾet-ʿeglôn... ʿal-yiśrāʾēl — 'YHWH strengthened Eglon against Israel.' The verb ḥizzēq (strengthen) — YHWH actively empowers the oppressor as instrument of discipline. The enemy's strength is not Israel's military failure but YHWH's sovereign appointment.
13: Eglon gathered Ammonites and Amalekites, defeating Israel and taking ʾîr hattĕmārîm (city of palms = Jericho). The symbolic weight: the first city of the conquest — taken by miracle — recaptured by Moabite coalition. The Jericho reversal measures the depth of Israel's regression from the Joshua ideal.
14: wayyaʿabdû bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl ʾet-ʿeglôn melek-môʾāb šĕmōneh ʿeśrēh šānāh — eighteen years of ʿābad (serve/worship) directed to the Moabite king. The covenant-service word that should go only to YHWH is directed to the enemy; Israel's refusal to serve YHWH produces servitude to the oppressor. The choice of whom to serve is never neutral.
15: wayyāqem YHWH lāhem môšîaʿ ʾet-ʾēhûd ben-gērāʾ ben-hayĕmînî ʾîš ʾiṭṭēr yad-yĕmînô — Ehud described as 'bound/restricted in his right hand' (ʾiṭṭēr yad-yĕmînô) = left-handed. The irony: a Benjaminite (ben-yāmîn = son of the right hand) who uses his left hand. His 'disability' — overlooked by the guards checking the standard sword-side — is the key to the assassination. YHWH consistently works through the unexpected and overlooked.
16: wayyaʿaś lô ʾēhûd ḥereb ûlāh šĕnê pîyôt gōmed ʾārkāh — a double-edged sword a gōmed (hapax legomenon, estimated 30-40 cm) in length. Šĕnê pîyôt (two edges, literally two mouths) — peh (mouth) used for a blade's edge is a common Semitic metaphor (cf. Heb 4:12 for a sword with two edges). The weapon's design — short, double-edged, concealable on the unexpected thigh — reflects deliberate planning.
17: wayyaqdēb ʾet-hamminḥāh lĕʿeglôn melek-môʾāb wĕʿeglôn ʾîš bārîʾ mĕʾōd — 'he presented the tribute to Eglon... Eglon was a very fat man.' The name ʿeglôn (round one, calf) from ʿēgel (calf) — a narrator's irony planted in the first description. Bārîʾ (fat, stout) is clinically observed; the same word for Pharaoh's fat cows (Gen 41:2). The detail functions as a plot mechanism: Eglon's obesity allows the blade to disappear into the wound (v.22).
18: After presenting the tribute Ehud sent away the bearers (wayyĕšallaḥ ʾet-hāʿām nōśĕʾê hamminḥāh). The deliberate pacing creates narrative suspense: preparation (sword made, v.16) → presentation (tribute offered, v.17) → entourage dismissed (v.18) → return alone (v.19). The narrator releases information at exactly the rate of the protagonist's plan.
19: wĕhûʾ šāb min-happĕsilîm ʾašer ʾet-haggilgāl — Ehud turned back from the pĕsilîm (carved idols/standing stones) at Gilgal. The idolatrous standing stones at the conquest's covenant-renewal site miniaturize the book's syncretism theme. dĕbar-sēter lî ʾēlêkā — 'I have a secret word for you' — sēter (hidden, secret) echoes prophetic reception language; Ehud uses dĕbar ʾĕlōhîm (word of God) in v.20 to gain private audience.
20: wĕhûʾ yôšēb bǎʿăliyyat hammĕqērāh ʾašer lô lĕbaddô — Eglon alone in his cool roof chamber. The privative lĕbaddô (alone, by himself) is the assassin's gift. dĕbar ʾĕlōhîm lî ʾēlêkā — 'I have a word of God for you.' Ehud appropriates the prophetic speech-formula (dĕbar ʾĕlōhîm) for lethal purpose; Eglon's respectful rising to receive the divine word places him in the posture the sword requires.
21: wayyišlaḥ ʾēhûd ʾet-yad haśśĕmōʾl wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-haḥereb mēʿal yĕrek yĕmînô wayyitqāʿehā bĕbiṭnô — 'Ehud reached with his left hand, took the sword from his right thigh, and thrust it into his belly.' The left hand is highlighted throughout as the operative agent. Guards searched the left thigh (normal sword-side); the right thigh carried no weapon expectation. The verb tāqaʿ (thrust, drive sharply) is onomatopoeic — a decisive, single motion.
22: wayyāṣēʾ happaršedōnāh — Ehud went out via happaršedōn. The word paršedōn is a hapax legomenon of contested meaning: 'the anteroom/vestibule,' or (following some interpreters) a term for the excretion caused by the wound. The double-hapax sequence (v.22-23) in a high-action scene may preserve archaic architectural vocabulary or reflect deliberate narrative distancing from the deed's gruesomeness through rare/opaque language.
23: wayyisgor daltôt hāʿăliyyāh baʿădô ûnāʿal — Ehud shut the roof-chamber doors behind him and locked them. The locked door is the mechanism that creates the servants' confusion and delay (v.24-25), enabling Ehud's escape. The narrative's precise attention to the door-locking detail anticipates its payoff in v.24.
24: ʾak mēsîk hûʾ ʾet-raglāyw baḥădar hammĕqērāh — 'surely he is relieving himself' (lit. 'covering his feet,' a euphemism for defecation). The servants' misreading of a locked door as their king on the toilet creates the time-delay that saves Ehud. The comic irony — Moabite servants imagining their king on the toilet while he lies dead — de-glamorizes royal pretension through scatalogical humor.
25: wayyāḥîlû ʿad-bôš — 'they waited until they were embarrassed' (bôš = shame, to be put to shame). The same verb describes the people's shame while Moses delayed on Sinai (Exod 32:1). The servants' shame-driven delay is Ehud's escape window. When propriety finally broke, they unlocked the doors with a key and found Eglon fallen dead on the floor.
26: wĕʾēhûd nimlāṭ bĕhitmāhĕmāham — 'Ehud escaped during their delay/hesitation.' The verb māhah (delay, tarry, hesitate) is the servants' fatal failure of initiative. He passes back through the pĕsilîm (idolatrous standing stones) on his escape route — the same contested spiritual geography he navigated on the way in.
27: wayyitqaʿ baššôpār bĕhar ʾeprayim wayyērĕdû ʾimmô bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl — 'he sounded the shofar in the hill country of Ephraim and the Israelites went down with him.' The šôpār (ram's horn trumpet) is the assembly signal (Judg 6:34; 1 Sam 13:3; Num 10:9) — here simultaneously announcing the oppressor's death and calling Israel's warriors. wĕhûʾ lipnêhem (with him at their head) — Ehud leads from the front.
28: kî nātan YHWH ʾet-ʾōyĕbêkem ʾet-môʾāb bĕyedkem — 'YHWH has given your enemies into your hand.' The proleptic perfect again: the victory declared complete before the decisive engagement. Ehud commands seizure of the Jordan fords (maʿabĕrôt hayyardēn lĕmôʾāb) to cut off Moabite retreat — tactical precision ensuring no escape. lōʾ nātĕnû ʾîš laʿăbōr — 'they let no man cross.'
29: wayyakkû ʾet-môʾāb baʿēt hahîʾ kaʿăśeret ʾălāpîm ʾîš kol-šāmēn wĕkol-ʾîš ḥāyil wĕlōʾ nimlāṭ ʾîš — 'they killed about ten thousand Moabites, all strong, all able-bodied men; not a man escaped.' Šāmēn (fat, well-fed) echoes bārîʾ for Eglon — the elite, prosperous Moabite military destroyed completely. Total victory (wĕlōʾ nimlāṭ ʾîš) signals full covenant restoration after oppression.
30: wayyikānaʿ môʾāb bayyôm hahûʾ taḥat yad yiśrāʾēl waytišqōṭ hāʾāreṣ šĕmōnîm šānāh — 'Moab was subdued; the land had rest eighty years.' Kānaʿ (be subdued, humbled) shares a root with Kĕnaʿan (Canaan) — the Canaanite subjugation vocabulary is applied to Moab. The eighty-year rest — double Othniel's period — perhaps reflects the completeness of this particular victory, or the proportionally longer recovery from the longer combined oppression (eight + eighteen = twenty-six years).
31: wayyak ʾet-pĕlištîm šēš-mēʾôt ʾîš bĕmalmad habbāqār — Shamgar killed six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad (malmad habbāqār). The malmad (pointed cattle-driving stick) is a farm implement, not a weapon — the Judges pattern of YHWH working through the unexpected means. gam-hûʾ hôšîaʿ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — 'he also delivered Israel.' The brief formula confers full deliverer status on Shamgar despite a single verse. The name ben-ʿănāt (son of Anat) suggests non-Israelite background — another outsider incorporated into Israel's salvation history.
